segments.s501-g9: سکندر is the proper name (subject). بپرسید is past-tense, 3rd-person singular (he asked); in classical/poetic Persian the b- prefix marks the past here. از marks the source/agent of the question (‘from’), and سرکشان is a plural noun meaning those who peep/watch (the onlookers). Word order is Subject Verb + prepositional source phrase ('Alexander asked from the onlookers').
segments.s501-g10: که is the complementizer introducing an indirect question ('what ...'). ایدر = ایـدر (here) is an adverb of place. چه is the interrogative 'what'. دارد is present-tense 'has' (used here in the sense ‘there is / contains’), forming the predicate. شگفتی نشان is a noun phrase 'marvel/wondrous sign/spectacle' (شگفتی modifies نشان); together the clause asks 'what wondrous sign is here.'
segments.s501-g11: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the speech verb. گفت here is the past-tense verb 'said' (classical usage), followed by با او 'to him' (prepositional phrase marking the indirect object). یکی ('a certain') precedes the noun مرد پیر ('old man'), forming 'a certain old man' as the speaker. Word order: adverb + verb + indirect object + indefinite subject.
segments.s501-g12: که‌ای here is the archaic/vocative construction (که + ای) used to address someone: 'O ...'. شاه is the addressee 'king'. نیک‌اختر is an epithet ('Nīk-akhter' = 'auspicious-star' or felicitous epithet) and شهرگیر is another epithet 'conqueror/sieger of cities'. This segment is a vocative phrase appended to the reported speech.
segments.s501-g13: یکی functions as an indefinite 'a certain' / existential marker introducing the existence of something. آبگیرست is a fused descriptive predicate meaning 'is a pool/reservoir' (آبگیر + است). زان ('from that' / 'on that') together with روی ('side/surface; here used as 'on the side of') and شهر ('city') form the locative phrase 'on that side of the city.' Word order: existential phrase + predicate + locative.
segments.s501-g14: کزان (= از + آن) is 'from which/of which' pointing back to the pool. آب is repeated as the substantive 'water'. کس را is an object phrase 'anyone / someone' with را as the definite/object marker/emphatic particle; here کس = 'any person'. ندیدیم is the 1st-person-plural past negative 'we did not see'. بهر ('for') is a preposition meaning 'for / toward' and here leaves the clause purposive or incomplete (poetic ellipsis), so the whole segment reads 'from which we have seen no one draw water for...'.
segments.s501-g15: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning 'when'. خورشید تابان is the noun phrase 'the shining sun' (noun + participial adjective). بدانجا is the adverb 'to that place / there', and رسید is the past-tense verb 'reached/arrived'. The clause is temporal: 'When the shining sun reached that place...'.
segments.s501-g16: بران ('thereon' / 'upon that') links back to the place reached; ژرف دریا is a noun phrase 'a deep sea' (ژرف qualifying دریا). شود is the present/future-like auxiliary 'becomes/is made' (here simple classical present/past-cohesion), and ناپدید is the predicative adjective/participle 'vanished/hidden/invisible'. The clause means 'a deep sea thereupon became invisible/vanished.'
segments.s501-g25: چنین is an adverb meaning “thus/so” placed before the verb; گفت is the verb ‘said/spoke’ (classical past/preterite), and روشن‌دل پر خرد is a compound epithet after the verb: روشن‌دل “clear-/bright-minded” modified by پر خرد “full of wisdom.” Word order (adverb + verb + epithet) is typical of epic/classical Persian inversion.
segments.s501-g26: که introduces the reported content (“that” / introducing the quotation). هرک is an indefinite relative ‘whoever.’ آب حیوان is a genitive/compound where آب is the head noun “water” and حیوان qualifies it (here idiomatically ‘living/animating’ or ‘the water of life’). خورد is the past of خوردن ‘to eat/drink’ (here ‘drinks’/‘has drunk’). کی is interrogative/adverb ‘when/ever’ and مرد here functions as past-form of مردن ‘(he) died’ (classical terse past), so the clause asks rhetorically when such a one will die.
segments.s501-g27: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from.’ فردوس is ‘Paradise.’ دارد is present (he/it has or there is) used here to mean ‘there is’ or ‘has.’ بران is a demonstrative/adverbial ‘to/onto that’ (pointing to the spring), and چشمه راه is ‘a path to the spring’ (چشمه = spring, راه = way/path). The word order (preposition + place + verb + demonstrative + noun) is typical of classical Persian compression.
segments.s501-g28: بشوید is a present/subjunctive/passive-form of شستن ‘to wash’ (here meaning ‘is washed’ or ‘may be washed’ in classical style). برآن means ‘upon/there’ (referring back to the spring). تن is ‘body.’ بریزد is a present/subjunctive/optative of ریختن ‘to pour/ fall off,’ here meaning ‘sin falls off / is shaken off.’ گناه is ‘sin.’ The two verbs describe effects taking place with respect to that spring: the body is cleansed and sin is cast off.
segments.s501-g29: بپرسید here is a reported-past form ‘asked’ (classical narrative form). پس = ‘then’ sequencing the action. شه = ‘the king’ (title). که introduces the dependent interrogative clause. تاریک جای is a noun phrase ‘dark place’ (تاریک = dark, جای = place).
segments.s501-g30: بدو = ‘to it / to him’ (here ‘to it,’ pointing to the place just named). اندرون = ‘inside/within.’ چون = ‘how / in what manner’ or ‘as’ (here interrogative ‘how’). رود in the original is a verbal form ‘goes’ (3rd person), although the provided noun senses do not reflect that; it functions as the verb of motion. چارپای = ‘four-footed (animal/beast),’ the subject of رود. The clause asks how a quadruped can enter or go into that interior.
segments.s501-g31: چنین = ‘thus.’ پاسخ آورد is a fixed classical phrase ‘brought an answer’ = ‘answered’ (آورد is the verb ‘brought/uttered,’ here used to mark he gave that reply). یزدان‌پرست is the agent ‘the pious one / God-worshipper’ who speaks. The phrase orders adverb + noun phrase + verb, a common narrative pattern.
segments.s501-g32: کزان = ‘from which / from that’ (relative/adverb referring to the path just mentioned). راه = ‘way / path.’ بر is the preposition ‘on/upon’ (here heading the verbal phrase ‘sit upon’). کره = ‘foal / colt’ (young horse) and باید نشست = modal + verb: باید ‘one must / it is necessary’ + نشست here is the verb ‘sit’ (classical infinitive/imperative sense ‘to sit/seat oneself’ or ‘mount’). Together: ‘from which path one must sit upon a colt,’ i.e. one must mount a colt to use that way.
segments.s501-g17: پس is a temporal/consequential adverb ('then'). چشمه‌در is the noun phrase 'the spring' (compound form); تیره (adjective 'dark') and گردد (verb 'become') form the predicate 'becomes dark'. The final جهان is left syntactically hanging here to be completed by the next clause (typical poetic ellipsis): two short clauses are linked across the line break.
segments.s501-g18: شود is the copular/phase verb 'becomes'. آشکارای is a poetic/derivational form of آشکارا (literally 'manifestation' or 'the manifest'), where the -ی turns the adverbial base into a noun-like element; گیتی نهان ('the hidden world/cosmos') is a genitival phrase modifying آشکارای. Together 'شود آشکارای گیتی نهان' = 'becomes the manifestation/revealed face of the hidden world.'
segments.s501-g19: وزان is the fused prepositional particle 'و + زان' = 'and/from that' (i.e., 'from that place'); جای تاریک is the noun phrase 'dark place'. چندان is a quantifier 'so much' modifying سخن ('speech/talk'), producing 'so much talk.' The order (prepositional phrase then quantified noun) is normal in classical poetic diction.
segments.s501-g20: شنیدم is the past tense 'I heard'. که introduces a reported clause. هرگز + نیاید is a negative temporal expression: هرگز 'never' and نیاید (present/subjunctive-form used here in narrative) = 'does not/will not come' or 'never comes'; به بن ('to the root/base') is the goal phrase, so 'never reaches the end/root' — i.e. 'never comes to an end.'
segments.s501-g21: خرد یافته is a compound adjective meaning 'having found / acquired wisdom' (خرد 'wisdom' + یافته 'found') and modifies مرد ('man'). یزدان‌پرست is an appositive adjective 'God‑worshipper / pious', so the whole phrase describes 'a wise, pious man.'
segments.s501-g22: بدو = 'to him' (dative); در یکی چشمه = 'at/in a certain spring' (در 'in/at', یکی used as 'a certain'); گوید here is the verb 'says' (the printed sense list gives nouns for this lemma, so the exact verbal sense is not listed) and که هست is the reported clause 'that it exists' (که introducing the content clause and هست the copula). Word order is typical: indirect object + circumstantial phrase + verb + reported clause.
segments.s501-g23: گشاده سخن مرد is a noun phrase 'the man of open/frank speech' (گشاده modifying سخن). با رای و کام is an instrumental/attitudinal phrase 'with counsel (rāy) and desire (kām)' describing his motive or disposition; با + noun phrase is the normal 'with/by means of' construction.
segments.s501-g24: همی is a classical continuous marker here ('kept / continually'); آب حیوانش is the object 'his/that water of life' (حیوانش = حیوان + -ش, 'its life/that which gives life'); خواند is the perfect/past verb 'called/named' (here 'to name/call'); به نام is the standard formula 'by the name' — together 'he continually named/called the living water by name.'
segments.s501-g1: بپرسید is the epic/past 3rd-person form with the archaic b- prefix meaning 'he asked/inquired'. The two noun phrases هرچیز و دریا are coordinated objects (هرچیز 'everything' and دریا 'the sea') and بدید is another past 3rd-person verb ('he saw'), sharing the same omitted subject (the narrative subject). The clause thus strings two past actions: he asked and he saw.
segments.s501-g2: وزان is a connective prepositional phrase 'and/from that' linking this clause to the previous one. روی لشکر is a verbless (nominal) phrase 'the face/face-like front of the host' (روی = face, لشکر = army) and به مغرب کشید uses کشید (here 'to draw/turn/move') with به مغرب 'toward the west' to mean 'the army turned westward'; the subject is nominal (the army) and the verb is past 3rd-person.
segments.s501-g3: یکی is the indefinite 'a certain' (introducing an item), شارستان 'city/town' is the noun, and پیشش آمد is a verbal phrase 'appeared/came before him' with آمد as past 3rd-person. بزرگ is an adjective 'great' placed after the verb phrase in poetic word order to modify شارستان (postpositive adjective is common in classical verse).
segments.s501-g4: بدو here functions as a locative/dative 'to it / in it / unto it' referring back to the city. اندرون 'within' introduces the inner location and مردمانی 'people' is the noun; سترگ is an attributive adjective (poetic/archaic) meaning 'great/mighty' modifying مردمانی. The copula is omitted (typical in epic diction), so the clause reads 'within it were mighty people.'
segments.s501-g5: The repeated همه ('all') gives distributive/emphatic force. روی سرخ and موی زرد are noun + adjective combinations ('faces red' / 'hair yellow') with the copula omitted (understood 'were'). The conjunction و links the two balanced noun phrases; this compact, adjectival description is typical of Ferdowsi's cataloging style.
segments.s501-g6: همه is again the distributed subject. در خور is a fixed classical expression meaning 'worthy/fit for' (here 'fit for war'), with جنگ as its object. روز نبرد is a time phrase 'the day of battle' specifying when; the copula ('were') is omitted and understood. Grammatically, در + noun + noun builds a prepositional/attributive phrase qualifying the subject.
segments.s501-g7: به فرمان is an instrumental phrase 'by (the) command/order.' به پیش سکندر شدند combines the directional prepositional phrase به پیش 'to the presence of' with سکندر (the addressee) and شد(ند) as the past 3rd-person plural verb 'they came/appeared.' The clause therefore means 'by order they came before Alexander.'
segments.s501-g8: دو تا گشته (here compressed in the text) expresses formation into two (two groups or ranks) with گشته as a perfect/participial form 'having become/turned.' The conjunction و links this to دست بر سر شدند, a light-verb construction where دست بر سر 'hands upon head' describes the gesture and شدند completes it ('they made themselves [hands] upon their heads' = 'they placed their hands on their heads' — a gesture of submission or lament). The sequence uses participial and auxiliary forms typical of narrative compression.
segments.s501-g33: به چوپان بفرمود is a dative + verb construction: به marks the indirect object 'to/for' and چوپان is 'the shepherd'. بفرمود is the past, honorific/epistolary third‑person verb 'he ordered/commanded'. The following noun phrase کاسپ یله is the content of that command (what he ordered), with یله a postposed adjective meaning 'unrestrained/unruly' modifying کاسپ (here 'the Caspian [people/flock]').
segments.s501-g34: سراسر is an adverb/quantifier 'entire/throughout'. به لشکرگه is a prepositional phrase 'into/to the army‑camp' (به = 'towards/into'). آرد is the 3rd person singular present/subjunctive form of آوردن used here after a verb of ordering — read as 'that he bring' or 'bring' in a commanded sense. گله is the direct object 'the flock/herd'; its post‑verbal position is a poetic/archaic word order (classical Persian poetry allows such inversions).
segments.s501-g35: گزین کرد is a simple past 'he chose/selected' (گزین + کرد). زو is a poetic contraction of ز او = 'from him/them' (ز = 'from'); it marks the source of what was chosen. بارگی here means 'burden‑bearers/pack‑animals/porters' (a noun), and the numeral phrase ده هزار follows the noun (classical/poetic word order can place the counted noun before its numeral), so بارگی ده هزار = 'ten thousand burden‑carriers'.
segments.s501-g36: همه چار سال is an adverbial time phrase: همه 'whole/entire' + چار سال 'four years' giving 'for the whole four years'. از در کارزار is a locative/causal phrase 'in/throughout the field of battle' (در کارزار = 'in battle'); combined the phrase indicates the duration/setting of service — 'throughout four years of fighting.'

sentences.s501-s5: Alexander asked the onlookers what wondrous sight this was here.
sentences.s501-s6: Thus spoke to him a certain old man: 'O king, auspicious-star and conqueror of cities.'
sentences.s501-s7: On that side of the city there is a reservoir of water; from which we have not seen anyone draw water for (a purpose).
sentences.s501-s8: When the shining sun reached that place, thereupon a deep sea became invisible.
sentences.s501-s13: Thus spoke the bright-minded, full of wisdom: “Whoever drinks the water of life — when will he die?”
sentences.s501-s14: From Paradise there is a way to that spring; the body is washed there and sin pours off it.
sentences.s501-s15: Then the king asked, “What is that dark place, and how does a four-footed beast go inside it?”
sentences.s501-s16: Thus the God-worshipper replied: from that path one must sit upon (mount) a colt.
sentences.s501-s9: Then the spring grew dark; the world became the revealed aspect of the hidden cosmos.
sentences.s501-s10: From that dark place I heard so much speech that it never comes to a close.
sentences.s501-s11: A wise, God‑worshipping man, having won wisdom, at a certain spring said to him that it exists.
sentences.s501-s12: The man of open speech, with counsel and desire, kept calling that water by name.
sentences.s501-s1: He inquired and saw everything and the sea, and from that the army turned its face to the west.
sentences.s501-s2: A great city appeared before him; within it were mighty people.
sentences.s501-s3: All had red faces and all had yellow hair; all were fit for war on the day of battle.
sentences.s501-s4: By command they came before Alexander; they formed into two groups and put their hands upon their heads.
sentences.s501-s17: He ordered the shepherd to bring the unruly (Caspian) flock entirely into the army camp.
sentences.s501-s18: From them he selected ten thousand burden‑carriers, for the whole four years in the fighting.
